title: Polyurethane Nanocomposites Containing Laminated Anisotropic Nanoparticles Derived from Organophilic Layered Silicates
abstract:
[11] a) T. Yamase, H. Naruke, J. Chem. Soc. Dalton Trans. 1991, 285; b) G.
Blasse, B. C. Grabmaier, Luminescent Materials, Springer, Berlin, 1994.
[12] G. Blasse, J. Chem. Phys. 1966, 45, 2356.
[13] A. J. de Vries, J. P. M. van Vliet, G. Blasse, Phys. Stat. Sol. (b) 1988,
149, 391.
Polyurethane Nanocomposites Containing
Laminated Anisotropic Nanoparticles Derived
from Organophilic Layered Silicates**
By Carsten Zilg, Ralf Thomann, Rolf Mülhaupt,* and
Jürgen Finter
Polyurethanes represent extraordinarily versatile poly-
meric materials which can be tailored to meet the highly di-
versified demands of modern technologies such as coatings,
adhesives, reaction injection molding, fibers, foams, rub-
bers, thermoplastic elastomers, and composites.[1±3] It is an
important objective in polyurethane development to im-
prove resistance against mechanical deformation without
sacrificing high elongation at break. Since the early pio-
neering advances by Otto Bayer,[4] polyurethane properties
are modified either by varying polyurethane microstruc-
ture, resulting from step-growth polymerization of isocya-
nate resins with polyols, or by dispersing inorganic and or-
ganic fillers within the polyurethane continuous matrix. A
wide variety of fillers, including clay and wollastonites, are
being applied in polyurethane formulations to reduce costs
and to reinforce the polyurethane matrix.[5] Properties of
filled polyurethanes are dependent upon filler shape, aver-
age diameter and interfacial coupling. Anisotropic particles
with a large length/diameter ratio (aspect ratio), e.g., whis-
kers and fibers, were found to be especially effective in ma-
trix reinforcement. Frequently, filler addition is accompa-
nied by increased strength and stiffness at the expense of
substantially reduced elongation at break.
Although nanoscale anisotropic particles are expected to
offer attractive potential for polyurethane reinforcement,
application of anisotropic nanofillers is restricted by the
limited availability of nanowhiskers, poor dispersion, and
handling problems associated with potential health hazards
resulting from inhalation of such particles. Therefore, sev-
eral attempts have been made to produce nanowhisker dis-
persions in polyols which are much easier to handle. For
example, N-(4-aminobenzoyl)-caprolactam was polymer-
ized at 200 C in dihydroxy-terminated poly(tetrahydrofur-
an) to produce dispersions of rigid polybenzamide nano-
whisker of approximately 2000 nm length and 200 nm
diameter. Interfacial coupling and steric stabilization were
achieved by reacting hydroxy end groups of the polyol with
N-acyl-lactam end groups located at the polybenzamide
whisker surface. Upon curing with diisocyanate, the result-
ing polyurethane nanocomposites exhibited the unusual
combination of increased tensile strength and Young's
modulus without sacrificing high elongation at break.[6,7]
Particle formation via sol/gel technology leads to disper-
sions of mainly isotropic nanoparticles in polyurethane.[8]
Recently, intercalation of organophilic layered silicates
with polymers has been introduced as an attractive route
leading to versatile polymer nanocomposites which contain
nanoscale layered silicates with high aspect ratio. Layered
silicates, e.g., clay, consist of anionically charged layers of
aluminum/magnesium
or
magnesium/lithium
silicates
where cations such as sodium, potassium, magnesium or
calcium are located in the interlayer galleries. They are ren-
dered organophilic when gallery cations are exchanged by
quaternary alkyl ammonium ions containing at least one
long alkyl chain. When polymers intercalate layered sil-
icates, interlayer distance increases and the silicates are
broken down into their nanoscale building blocks to afford
dispersions of anisotropic inorganic/organic hybrid parti-
cles with high aspect ratios. This technology was reviewed
by Lagaly,[9] Akelah,[10] Pinnavaia et al.,[11] and Gianne-
lis.[12] Thermoplastic and thermosetting nanocomposites
were reported to exhibit unusual properties such as im-
proved stiffness/strength/toughness balance, improved heat
distortion temperature, barrier properties, and flame retar-
dency.[13] A few examples describing use of special interca-
lated organophilic clays in polyurethane formulation are
disclosed in the patent literature. Janoski applied organo-
clay in which a quaternary long chain fatty amine is ioni-
cally bonded to clay, which was used as filler, thickening
agent and compatibilizer in asphaltic polyurethane disper-
sions to enhance dispersion of asphalt. Such dispersions
were used as adhesives in roofing applications.[14] In an-
other patent, modified polyurethanes were obtained by
fluidizing fillers such as sand, talc, and clay in polyols and
subsequent crosslinking with isocyanate.[15] Miyanaga and
Tsunoda reported isocyanuration catalysts, composed of
layered silicates ion-exchanged with organic ammonium
compounds, for preparing polyisocyanurate-layered silicate
nanocomposites.[16]
The objective of this research was to prepare polyur-
ethane nanocomposites containing intercalated synthetic
layered silicates and to examine morphology development
and basic structure/property correlations of multiphase
polyurethanes. In this research, the layered silicate was
composed of synthetic fluoromica (trade name SOMASIF
ME100), which is available by heating Na2SiF6 with talc.
As shown in Figure 1, the layers consist of aluminosilicates

where a sheet of octahedral alumina is sandwiched in be-
tween two sheets of tetrahedral fluorosilica. Since part of
Al3+ in the central octahedral sheet is substituted by Mg2+,
the layers are anionically charged and contain Na+ as a
counterion located in the interlayer gallery. Similar to pro-
cedures reported by Giannelis and others for smectites,[12]
this fluoromica was swollen with water and Na+ was ex-
changed with bis(hydroxyethyl)-methyl-dodecylammonium
chloride (trade name Ethoquad C12) in order to produce
organophilic mica, which precipitated and was filtered off.
The dried organophilic mica was dispersed in trihydroxy-
terminated oligo(propyleneoxide) with number average
molar mass of 3800 g/mol by means of a high shear mixer.
Stable transparent polyol dispersions were obtained. This
polyoldispersion
was
cured
with
diisocyanatophenyl
methane (55 mol-% / 45 mol-% mixture of 4,4¢- and 4,2¢-
isomers), accelerated with 0.6 wt. -% N,N-dimethyl-benzyl-
amine at 80 C to afford polyurethane nanocomposites. In
comparison to conventional filled polyurethane, the cure
reaction during nanocomposite formation is slowed down
slightly. Most likely, intercalated mica is interacting and
partially deactivating the tertiary amine accelerator. As is
apparent from wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) analy-
sis, displayed in Figure 2, the average interlayer spacing in-
creased from 0.9 nm, typical for non-modified fluoromica,
to 8.8 nm in the case of the polyurethane nanocomposites.
Obviously, both polyol and polyurethane were able to in-
tercalate the three-layered aluminosilicate, which was com-
patibilized by means of surface functionalization with
bis(hydroxyethyl)methyldodecylammonium ions.
Morphology development in polyurethane nanocompo-
sites was imaged by means of transmission electron micros-
copy (Fig. 3) on thin sections which were cut at tempera-
tures of ±110 C. During dispersion, the fairly large
isotropic
mica
particles
of
average
diameter
around
Fig. 1. Layered fluoromica (A) and the cor-
responding polyurethane nanocomposites
containing intercalated (B) and exfoliated
(C) silicate layers.
Fig. 2. WAXS of fluoromica (A) and the corresponding polyurethane nano-
composite (B) containing 10 wt. -% of fluoromica which was ion exchanged
with bis(2-hydroethyl)methyldodecylammonium chloride.
Fig. 3. Transmission electron microscopic images of polyurethane nanocom-
posite, NC2.

where a sheet of octahedral alumina is sandwiched in be-
tween two sheets of tetrahedral fluorosilica. Since part of
Al3+ in the central octahedral sheet is substituted by Mg2+,
the layers are anionically charged and contain Na+ as a
counterion located in the interlayer gallery. Similar to pro-
cedures reported by Giannelis and others for smectites,[12]
this fluoromica was swollen with water and Na+ was ex-
changed with bis(hydroxyethyl)-methyl-dodecylammonium
chloride (trade name Ethoquad C12) in order to produce
organophilic mica, which precipitated and was filtered off.
The dried organophilic mica was dispersed in trihydroxy-
terminated oligo(propyleneoxide) with number average
molar mass of 3800 g/mol by means of a high shear mixer.
Stable transparent polyol dispersions were obtained. This
methane (55 mol-% / 45 mol-% mixture of 4,4¢- and 4,2¢-
isomers), accelerated with 0.6 wt. -% N,N-dimethyl-benzyl-
amine at 80 C to afford polyurethane nanocomposites. In
comparison to conventional filled polyurethane, the cure
reaction during nanocomposite formation is slowed down
slightly. Most likely, intercalated mica is interacting and
partially deactivating the tertiary amine accelerator. As is
apparent from wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) analy-
sis, displayed in Figure 2, the average interlayer spacing in-
creased from 0.9 nm, typical for non-modified fluoromica,
to 8.8 nm in the case of the polyurethane nanocomposites.

tercalate the three-layered aluminosilicate, which was com-
patibilized by means of surface functionalization with
bis(hydroxyethyl)methyldodecylammonium ions.

sites was imaged by means of transmission electron micros-
copy (Fig. 3) on thin sections which were cut at tempera-
tures of ±110 C. During dispersion, the fairly large
Fig. 1. Layered fluoromica (A) and the cor-
responding polyurethane nanocomposites
containing intercalated (B) and exfoliated
Fig. 2. WAXS of fluoromica (A) and the corresponding polyurethane nano-
composite (B) containing 10 wt. -% of fluoromica which was ion exchanged
with bis(2-hydroethyl)methyldodecylammonium chloride.
Fig. 3. Transmission electron microscopic images of polyurethane nanocom-
5000 nm are broken down into much smaller anisotropic
nanoparticles resembling short fiber reinforcement of
500 nm length and 20±50 nm diameter (aspect ratio 10±20),
which correspond to structure B in Figure 1. Such nanopar-
ticles are composed of intercalated mica where inorganic
aluminosilicate layers are laminated with polyurethane.
Complete exfoliation (structure C in Fig. 1) was not ob-
served. The laminated nanoparticles are very well dis-
persed within the polyurethane matrix. This superstructure
formation is very similar to preferred morphological hierar-
chies observed by Akelah et al. for poly(butadiene-co-acry-
lonitrile)/montmorillonite nanocomposites, where layers,
laminated with polymer, assembled into multiplets of about
5 nm size.[17] In the case of polyurethane nanocomposites
the much better dispersion is evidenced by the presence of
individual nanoparticles containing a large number of alu-
minosilicate layers laminated with polyurethane. At pres-
ent the content of intercalated layered silicate was limited
to 10 wt. -% due to dispersion and processing problems re-
lated to viscosity build-up which was encountered at higher
The mechanical properties of polyurethanes are listed in
Table 1. Conventional polyurethanes, filled with non-modi-
fied mica (F samples), were compared to polyurethane
nanocomposites containing the equivalent amount of the
ion-exchanged organophilic mica (NC samples in Table 1).
The polyol/isocyanate ratio was kept constant in order to
study the role of the addition of layered aluminosilicates.
In contrast to conventional mica-filled polyurethanes, poly-
urethane nanocomposites exhibit substantially higher elon-
gation at break combined with much higher tensile
strength. There exists a linear correlation between weight
fraction of ion-exchanged mica and tensile strength as well
as elongation at break when the content of ion-exchanged
mica was varied between 2.5 and 8 wt. -%. In the case of
polyurethane nanocomposite both Shore A hardness and
Young's modulus, which reflect polymer stiffness, de-
creased slightly, whereas non-modified mica gave increased
stiffness and only small increases of tensile strength with in-
creasing filler content. Most likely, the laminated nanopar-
ticles are rather soft with respect to the pure inorganic
component. Unusual matrix reinforcement and tear resis-
tance of the nanocomposite with respect to the conven-
tional filled polyurethane may result from orientation of
the laminated anisotropic nanoparticles and excellent inter-
facial coupling between intercalated mica and the polyur-
ethane matrix resulting from reactions of isocyanate groups
with hydroxy groups of the alkyl ammonium ions bonded
ionically to the mica surface. Further studies on the role of
isocyanaurate formation, network density, interfacial com-
positions, and micromechanical processes, taking place dur-
ing deformation, will be conducted. In conclusion, interca-
lation and supramolecular assembly of layered inorganic
compounds represents an attractive route to polyurethane
nanocomposites which are reinforced by means of aniso-
tropic laminated nanoparticles consisting of inorganic
layers bonded together with polyurethane.
The synthetic fluoromica (Somasif ME 100) was purchased from CO-OP
Chemical Co., Japan. The organophilic fluoromica was prepared by ion ex-
changing Somasif ME100 with bis(2-hydroxyethyl)methyldodeceylammo-
nium chloride (Ethoquad C12 from Akzo) in aqueous phase. Thus 77.5 g
(240 mmol) bis(2-hydroxyethyl)methyldodeceylammonium chloride was
dissolved in 2000 ml of boiling, deionized water. Then 100 g of Somasif ME
100 was added slowly and the resulting suspension was stirred for 1 h. The
product was filtered off and washed with hot water until no chloride was de-
tected when adding 0.1 N AgNO3. The product was dried in a vented oven
for 48 h at 80 C and then in vacuum for 72 h at 80 C.
The polyol dispersion containing 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 wt. -% of the ion-ex-
changed fluoromica was prepared by dispersing the corresponding amount
of fluoromica in trihydroxy-terminated oligo(propyleneoxide) with number
average molar mass of 3800 g/mol (Desmophen PU 21-IK-01 from Bayer
AG) at 100 C and 3 mbar pressure using a Molteni Planimax high shear
mixer to remove traces of water. Then 0.6 g of N,N-dimethyl-benzylamine
(Desmorapid DB from Bayer AG) was added to 100 g of the polyol disper-
sion. After cooling to room temperature, this mixture was blended together
with 8.8 g diisocyanatodiphenyl-methane isomers (55 wt. -% 2,4-isomer and
45 wt. -% 4,4¢ isomer, isocyanate functionality 2,0, Baymidur KL 3-5002
from Bayer AG). After stirring for 3 min at room temperature and applying
3 mbar pressure to remove air bubbles, the resulting mixture was poured
into a mold (200 ´ 200 ´ 4 mm). Curing was performed at 80 C for 24 h in a
vented oven and yielded polyurethane nanocomposites (NC samples,
Table 1). The fluoromica-filled polyurethanes (F samples, Table 1) were
prepared according to the same procedures.
The degree of swelling and interlayer distance of the fluoromica was
studied by means of wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) using a Siemens
D500 apparatus with Cu Ka radiation (1.5418 ), and a scanning rate of
0.3 /min. The morphology was examined by means of transmission electron
microscopy measurements on thin section which were prepared by cutting
at ±110 C using an Ultracut E, Reichert & Jung ultramicrotome equipped
with a diamond knife. Mechanical properties such as Young's modulus, ten-
sile strength, and elongation at break were measured by means of stress/
strain tests according to ISO 527T2/93 using a crosshead speed of 100 mm/
min. Shore hardness A was measured according to ISO 868/85.

Morphokinetics: Growth of Mesoporous Silica
By San Ming Yang, Hong Yang, Neil Coombs,
Igor Sokolov, Charles T. Kresge, and Geoffrey A. Ozin*
The discovery that micellar and lyotropic liquid-crystal
phases direct the assembly of periodic mesoporous silica[1]
heralded to chemistry a new class of inorganic materials
with a hierarchical structure and complex form.[2] There-
after, syntheses emerged for mesostructured transition
metal oxides,[3] metal phosphates,[4] metal sulfides,[5] and
metals[6] with feature sizes in the range of 2±10 nm, while
block copolymers,[7] emulsions,[8] and colloidal latex crystal
arrays[9] as templates expanded pore sizes beyond 10 nm
up to micrometers. Syntheses of mesoporous silica films,[10]
fibers, curved shapes,[11] and patterns[12] created new oppor-
tunities for diverse applications. Relations established be-
tween synthesis conditions, mesostructure, and morphology
revealed the key role that defects play in the morphogen-
esis of mesoporous silica.[13,14] Although empirical models
and studies of the formation of mesoporous silica powders
have been presented,[15±17] none focuses on growth kinetics
toward specific shapes. Herein we describe the first such ki-
netic study that focuses attention on the nucleation and
growth of mesoporous silica fibers and gyroids. Images of
the smallest objects implicated in the creation of these
shapes are obtained by tapping-mode atomic force micros-
copy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM).
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) provides temporal infor-
mation on the size evolution of the growth objects, whereas
mass yield synthesis profiles define induction and growth
stages. Increasing pH correlates with slower nucleation and
a shape transition from fibers to gyroids. The smallest ob-
servable growth object in the emergence of these mesopor-
ous silica shapes is a soft 50±70 nm silicate liquid-crystal
In order to establish a model for the formation of meso-
porous silica shapes, it is necessary to elucidate the struc-
ture of the growth objects (e.g., micelle, silicate micelle, sili-
cate liquid-crystal seed), the nucleation mechanism (e.g.,
homogeneous micelle±silicate coassembly, collection of sili-
cate micelles), and the growth process (e.g., accretion of sil-
icate micelles on silicate liquid-crystal aggregates, floccula-
tion of silicate liquid-crystal aggregates). Because small
changes in local acidity can drive shape transitions in this
system,[10±14] it is especially important to understand the ef-
fect of pH on nucleation and growth rates for representa-
tive fiber and gyroid morphologies. This knowledge is es-
mesoporous materials with controlled morphologies.
(CTACl) was the surfactant template and tetraethyl orthosi-
licate (TEOS) was the silica precursor. Predominantly fiber
(x = 7) and gyroid (x = 3.5) shapes are formed in syntheses
based on the reagent ratios: 100 H2O : x HCl : 0.11 CTACl :
0.13 TEOS run at 20 C. Reagents were mixed for 3 min, fil-
tered, and placed immediately in a thermostatic DLS quartz
cuvette to give clear quiescent solutions. This procedure
took ~7±10 min to complete. The intensity of the autocorre-
lation function as a function of time was found for pH condi-
tions that favored the formation of gyroid shapes. This yields
diffusion coefficients and provides information on the time
evolution of the hydrodynamic radii of the gyroids by using
the Stokes±Einstein equation (Fig. 1). The CTACl micellar
species grows by ~1 nm during the induction stage. This slow
step in the acid synthesis most likely involves the gradual in-
corporation of protonated silicate into the trimethylammo-
nium head-group region of the micelle. Beyond this portion
